commit.name: Weather Card
commit.description: Data display with props, design tokens, and responsive layout
commit.prompt:
Build a weather card component. It should display:
- Current temperature (large, prominent)
- Weather condition with an appropriate icon/emoji (sunny, cloudy, rainy, etc.)
- Humidity percentage
- Wind speed
- A 5-day forecast strip at the bottom showing day name, icon, and high/low temps

All data must come from props — never hardcode weather data. Use CSS variables from the design system for all colors and spacing.

issues.description: The forecast items are static and lack hover states or visual indicators of interactivity.
issues.fix: Add a subtle hover background or scale effect to the forecast day columns.

issues.description: The current weather icon/condition lacks a descriptive aria-label, and the forecast icons rely on generic placeholders for ARIA labels.
issues.fix: Dynamically map icon strings to meaningful text (e.g., 'Sunny', 'Cloudy') for aria-labels, rather than repeating the current weather condition for all forecast days.

issues.description: The high/low temperatures in the forecast strip are stacked vertically which might feel cluttered.
issues.fix: Consider a side-by-side layout or consistent styling for the temperature range.

evaluations.critique: The component is clean, well-structured, and adheres strictly to the provided design system tokens. It effectively utilizes props to render a complete weather dashboard; minor improvements in aria-label accuracy and adding micro-interactions to the forecast strip would elevate this to production-ready status.

evaluation.critique: The component has good data binding and hierarchy, but suffers from inconsistent design token usage—some colors use variables while others have hardcoded fallbacks, and spacing relies on magic numbers (24, 32px) instead of ggui spacing tokens. The forecast strip could benefit from hover states and better responsive behavior on smaller screens.

commit.name: Interactive Periodic Table
commit.description: CSS Grid periodic table with category coloring, search, and detail panel
commit.prompt:
Build an interactive periodic table of elements.

Layout:
- CSS Grid: 18 columns. Place each element using gridRow and gridColumn from its data.
- Each cell: symbol (large, centered), atomic number (small, top-left)
- Color-code by category using design tokens (e.g., noble-gas → one color, halogen → another)
- Search input at top — filters by name or symbol (case-insensitive)
- Click an element → detail panel below grid shows: name, symbol, atomicNumber, atomicWeight, category
- Legend showing each category with its color

All element data comes from props.elements. Use design system CSS variables.

issues.description: The legend is hardcoded with a subset of categories instead of dynamically generating from the passed elements, potentially missing categories if data changes.
issues.fix: Derive the legend categories dynamically from the 'elements' prop using a Set to extract unique categories.

issues.description: Grid cells have low contrast between the text and some background colors (e.g., transition-metal or bright category colors).
issues.fix: Implement a conditional text color logic to switch between white/black based on the background color brightness.

issues.description: The grid elements are not properly labeled for screen readers beyond just the visual content.
issues.fix: Add 'aria-label' to the gridcell elements describing the element name and category for better screen reader context.

evaluations.critique: The component is well-structured and follows the layout requirements accurately. It handles the search filtering and interactivity gracefully, though it would benefit from more robust accessibility labels and dynamic legend generation.

evaluation.critique: The component has solid structure and good data rendering, but suffers from inconsistent design token usage with hardcoded fallback values (e.g., 'var(--ggui-spacing-6, 24px)') that undermine the design system approach. The grid layout works well for the periodic table, but polish is missing on interactive elements—the search input and grid cells lack visible hover/focus states and smooth transitions.
